title: Your Brain Never Stops Rewriting Your Memories
knowledge: Every time you recall a memory, your brain rewrites it slightly. This process, called memory reconsolidation, was discovered by neuroscientist Karim Nader in 2000. Each retrieval makes the memory slightly less accurate — meaning your oldest, most-replayed memories may be the furthest from what actually happened.

title: Your Skeleton Is Not the One You Were Born With
knowledge: Bone remodeling is a continuous process where osteoclasts break down old bone tissue and osteoblasts build new bone in its place. Dr. Harold Frost first described this cycle in the 1960s. Your entire skeleton is replaced roughly every 10 years, though the rate slows as you age.

title: You Are Not Actually Seeing Reality Right Now
knowledge: Your visual cortex receives incomplete data from your eyes and fills in the gaps using prediction and past experience. Neuroscientist David Eagleman calls this 'controlled hallucination.' There is a physical blind spot where the optic nerve exits the eye — your brain invisibly patches it with invented pixels every moment.

title: Your Heart Actually Stops When You Sneeze
knowledge: A sneeze generates a massive intrathoracic pressure spike that can briefly disrupt the heart's electrical rhythm, causing a compensatory pause. While the heart does not fully stop in most cases, the rhythm resets. Cardiologist John Fontaine confirmed this is a real physiological phenomenon.

title: Your Stomach Has Its Own Brain
knowledge: The enteric nervous system — a network of 500 million neurons lining your gut — operates largely independently from your brain. Neuroscientist Michael Gershon coined the term 'second brain' in his 1998 book. Your gut produces about 95% of the body's serotonin, directly influencing mood and emotional regulation.
